turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Responding to Feedback or a Performance Review

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Hi, thank you for meeting with me. I wanted to talk about the feedback on my presentation, because I’ve been thinking about it a lot.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Of course. Overall, your presentation had strong content and clear structure.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Thank you. I was worried because while speaking, I felt like my mind went blank sometimes.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That happens to many people. One thing we noticed was your pacing.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Pacing… Do you mean I was too fast or too slow?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Mostly too fast in the middle section.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: That’s interesting, because I thought I was being slow.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: In the beginning you were, but then you sped up.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Ah, I see. I think I was watching the time and panicking.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That makes sense. Time pressure often affects pacing.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: When you say “slow down”, what should I actually do? Because telling myself “slow down” doesn’t work.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s a good question. One concrete thing is to pause between ideas.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Pause meaning silence?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Yes, short silence.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: I always feel silence is awkward, like people will judge me.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Many people feel that way, but listeners usually appreciate pauses.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: I never thought about listener’s perspective. I only think about myself when presenting.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s very common, especially in a second language.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: What about eye contact? You mentioned it briefly in the written feedback.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Yes. You relied on your slides a bit too much.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: I thought looking at slides is respectful. But maybe it looks like I’m avoiding audience?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: It can give that impression, yes.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay. So I should look at audience even if I feel uncomfortable?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: You don’t need constant eye contact, just brief moments.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Alright. Another thing I worry about is pronunciation.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Your pronunciation was mostly clear.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Mostly? So sometimes not clear?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: There were a few words, but context helped.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Context meaning the sentence helped people guess?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay, that makes me feel better. I was worried people didn’t understand anything.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s not the case at all.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: If I want to improve pronunciation, what should I focus on first?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Stress and intonation rather than individual sounds.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Stress meaning which word is stronger?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay. That feels more manageable.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Would you like to set a goal for your next presentation?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes. My goal is to pause more, speak slower, and not panic about time.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s a very realistic goal.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Thank you. Talking about this in detail really helps.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: I’m glad. You’re making steady progress.
